data.title: AI agents won't kill credit cards, but they will open new avenues.
data.description:
Author: Noah Levine Source: a16zcrypto Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

A Wrong War

A few weeks ago, an article by Citrini Research claimed that stablecoins would bypass Visa and Mastercard, causing card organization stock prices to plummet and the crypto community to cheer. This logic sounds perfect: AI intelligence optimizes every transaction, exchange fees are a kind of "tax," and stablecoins can bypass them. I'm in the crypto industry every day and wish this were true, but it's mostly wrong. It's not because stablecoins aren't important, but because the real opportunity doesn't lie in replacing bank cards, but in serving merchants who have difficulty integrating into the bank card system.

In most scenarios, bank cards will win. Citrini's conclusion is based on the assumption that AI agents, freed from human habits, will optimize to bypass exchange fees. However, bank cards are more than just money transfer tools; they also provide unsecured credit, pre-authorization for uncertain transactions, and fraud protection through chargeback rights. Stablecoins can transfer funds, but currently cannot do the remaining things. For example, your AI agent books a hotel for you, but the actual hotel is completely different from the pictures. With a bank card, you can directly initiate a dispute; with stablecoins, once the money is transferred, it cannot be reversed. 82% of Americans hold rewarded credit cards, with 18 billion cards in circulation globally. For the vast majority of transactions, consumers will not voluntarily give up transaction protection and points to choose an irreversible payment method with no benefits. Fraud detection further amplifies the advantage: card organizations run models covering billions of transactions in real time, while stablecoins currently lack a comparable network-level anti-fraud layer. Small payments are often considered a weakness of bank cards, but bank card networks have long been adapted to mismatched transaction scenarios. Visa, by aggregating daily settlements, has processed over 2 billion transportation tickets. The bank card industry has never given up on any transaction category; they always invent new products to dominate it. There's also an identity objection: "Agents cannot hold bank cards." But an agent is simply a new device. Your phone, watch, and computer all hold independent tokens pointing to the same card, just like Apple Pay technology. Your phone has never undergone KYC; it simply carries your token, and the same applies to agents. Visa has issued over 16 billion tokens, and agents will also receive these tokens. Visa's Smart Commerce framework is being piloted, Mastercard Agent Pay is available to cardholders nationwide, Stripe and OpenAI's Smart Agent Commerce protocol has been integrated into Etsy, and over a million Shopify merchants are about to join. For existing merchants and consumers, bank cards are likely to dominate Smart Agent e-commerce. The opportunity for stablecoins lies elsewhere: serving merchants who don't yet exist. Every platform migration creates a new batch of merchants that existing payment systems cannot serve. When eBay was founded, individual sellers couldn't obtain merchant accounts; PayPal served them, growing to over a million users and processing 40% of eBay's auction payments by 2000. Shopify grew from 42,000 merchants to 5.5 million in 13 years. As Alex Rampell and James da Costa pointed out, when Stripe was founded, many of its future customers didn't even exist. The pattern remains consistent: winners serve merchants that existing institutions can't cover. The AI ​​wave is likely creating these merchants faster than any previous platform migration. Last year alone, 36 million new developers joined GitHub. In the YC 2025 winter batch, a quarter of the companies had over 95% of their codebases generated by AI. On the popular AI coding platform Bolt.new, 67% of its 5 million users are not developers. People who couldn't write production-ready code two years ago are now releasing software. Everyone is a new buyer of developer infrastructure, purchasing via command line rather than sales communication. These developers are also new sellers. A "climate developer's" workflow requires various services: data interfaces, testing infrastructure, and deployment tools. Anyone with the same tools can develop and sell these services. The same force is creating both buyers and sellers simultaneously. Imagine this: an ambient developer spends four hours using AI tools to create a tool that displays the financial data of a publicly traded company—no website, no terms of service, no legal entity. Another developer's AI agent calls it 40,000 times a week, earning $0.10 each time, generating $40 in revenue, without any human access to the checkout page. I see ambient developers creating these kinds of tools every week, and their first question is always: How do I collect payment? For most people, the current answer is: No. Existing payment service providers struggle to integrate these types of merchants, not because of technical limitations, but because once a payment service provider approves a merchant's application, they bear the merchant's risk. If the merchant commits fraud or generates a large number of chargebacks, the payment service provider has to cover it. Applicants who cannot be insured are directly rejected. A tool without a website, a registered entity, or records is extremely difficult to pass underwriting audits. The system functions normally as designed, but it wasn't originally designed for this type of scenario. Payment providers can adapt; they've done it before, creating new risk levels for payment service providers and platforms. But it took 16 years from PayPal's inception to the industry's first underwriting guideline for the merchant aggregator model it pioneered. And these new merchants now need to receive payments. For them, accepting stablecoins is like a street vendor only accepting cash. Not because cash is better, but because merchants with this profile have historically struggled to pass bank card acceptance audits. In this gap, stablecoins are currently the only viable option, even if the wallet experience is still rudimentary and the compliance framework is still developing. Protocols like x402 have embedded stablecoin payments directly into HTTP requests, requiring no merchant account, no payment processor, no onboarding, and no liability for chargebacks. This doesn't require anyone to acknowledge that stablecoins are better than bank cards; it only requires that existing payment providers haven't adapted yet. These merchants aren't choosing between stablecoins and bank cards; they're choosing between stablecoins and "not receiving payments." What will emerge? Every wave of new merchants will eventually be absorbed by traditional payment systems, and this time will likely be no different—it's just a matter of time. But merchants always emerge first, followed by the underwriting system. In the gap between these two moments, stablecoins are the infrastructure. Bank cards serve every merchant that payment providers can underwrite; stablecoins serve every merchant that payment providers cannot. The next wave of e-commerce will emerge from this gap.

data.title: A massive talent shake-up at Alibaba: Is Alibaba's AI big model doomed?
data.description:
Author: Biteye; Source: X, @BiteyeCN

Just after the Lantern Festival, the Tongyi Qianwen team experienced a major reshuffle of core personnel: Technical lead Lin Junyang resigned, along with three other key figures: Yu Bowen, head of Qwen post-training; Hui Binyuan, head of Qwen Code; and Li Kaixin, a core contributor to Qwen 3.5 & VL & Coder.

This is not just an ordinary departure of a technical lead, but a systemic conflict concerning organizational structure, resource allocation, and open-source strategy. Biteye attempts to reconstruct the full picture of this personnel earthquake and ask a more fundamental question: In the AI ​​era, how should large companies truly place their technological ideals?

...

I. Blood Loss Overnight: Core Staff Depart en masse

Less than 24 hours after the release of the Qwen 3.5 small model, which had just been praised by Musk for its "amazing intelligence density," Lin Junyang, the technical head of Alibaba's Tongyi Qianwen, posted a brief farewell message on X in the early morning:

Head of Qwen: Appointed head of Tongyi Qianwen in 2022, and promoted to Alibaba's youngest P10 in 2025 at the age of 32.

Three others followed him. Yu Bowen, the head of training at Qwen, also left the company. A few hours later, Hui Binyuan, the head of Qwen Code, posted "me too" and changed his profile to former Qwen.

  • A few hours later, Kaixin Li, a core contributor to Qwen3.5 & VL & Coder, also announced his resignation and changed his Twitter profile to "Pre Qwen". This once-star team, which had created over 1 billion downloads globally, over 200,000 derivative models, and consistently ranked first among open-source large models, seems to be crumbling at a visible pace. II. Reasons: The Game Between Individuals and Large Companies in the AI ​​Era A tweet from Qwen team member @cherry_cc12 revealed just the tip of the iceberg of this turmoil. As information from internal meetings gradually leaks out, we attempt to piece together the full picture of this mass exodus.

    2.1 Organizational Dilemma: From Special Forces to Assembly Line

    The editor speculates that the original Qwen Lab was a sharp team of tech geeks, each a special forces soldier, a jack-of-all-trades. Lin Junyang was like a reinforced company commander, leading everyone into battle. However, rumors circulate online that the Qwen team plans to split up, transforming from a "vertically integrated" system covering different training processes and modalities into separate horizontally divided teams for pre-training, post-training, text, multimodal, etc. This is actually how traditional internet companies operate. Alibaba probably thought: Qwen Labs was initially an internally incubated project, but things changed after the New Year; they wanted to start applying the incubated project on a large scale. How to improve efficiency? By breaking down each step into Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs), improving the efficiency of each step, and thus improving overall efficiency. This idea is definitely outdated. If you look at how much effort OpenClaw put into it, you'll know that the game has truly changed in the AI ​​era. 2.2 The Resource Dilemma: Is it there or not? On one hand, "Qwen is the most important thing for the group," and on the other hand, Wu Ma says, "Resources are hard to satisfy everyone." This contradictory statement is very much like a leader who makes empty promises but never delivers. What does it mean that "Qwen is the top priority"? What does it mean that "the Chinese CEO has done his best"? What does it mean that resource bottlenecks are due to "information transmission problems"? Who are you trying to fool? There are only two possibilities. First: The upper management doesn't actually value Qwen that much; investing in Qwen is just an investment in AI FOMO (Fear of Missing Out). Second: The upper management is divided into two factions: one values ​​it and the other doesn't. The faction that doesn't value it starts all sorts of bottlenecks. In short, there must be a portion of the upper management who only pay lip service to it. Therefore, the product line, supposedly the highest priority, can't even secure basic resource guarantees. 2.3 The Game Between Individuals and Platforms: Who Can Rise Above the Organization? The most disheartening part of the leaked information is the HR's statement: "We can't elevate someone to a pedestal; the company can't accept irrational demands to retain them at any cost." Is this HR right? AI companies are already fiercely competing for talent: In 2024, Zhou Chang, the former technical leader of Qwen, left to start his own business and then quietly joined ByteDance's Seed team, receiving a "sky-high offer" of a 4-2 level position plus an eight-figure annual salary. In 2025, Meta offered a staggering $200 million compensation package to snatch Pang Ruoming from Apple, including not only high stock options but also milestone incentives directly linked to technological breakthroughs. Didn't this HR do any competitor research? Is this HR wrong? This statement seems to contain a millennia-old Chinese philosophy: the individual cannot be above the organization. 2.4 Political Struggle: Whose Party Are You On? Internally, it's said that "political factors weren't considered at all," but then it's said that "we need to consider where to put Zhouhao for maximum efficiency." This is quite interesting. The subtext seems to be that Zhouhao absolutely must be in this organization; the only question is where. Anyone who's watched palace dramas knows that who gets things done isn't important; who obeys is. To put it bluntly, for most professional managers, whether someone can actually solve problems and whether that person threatens their position are two equally important things. In a startup, you can jump as high as you want; in a large company, the security of those above you might be more important than your ability. Think about it carefully. 2.5 The Mismatch Between Open Source and Commercialization A deeper tension stems from the misalignment between open source and commercial paths. Qwen has established a huge reputation in the global open source community – its download volume, derivative models, and international recognition are all quite high. However, open source doesn't bring users and revenue. Now that Qwen has grown large, the group naturally asks, "I've invested so much, shouldn't you give me some return?" III. Reflection: The AI ​​Dilemma of Large Companies Actually, this happening at Alibaba is not surprising at all. Have you seen the skit "The Annual Meeting Can't Stop"? It's a script based on Alibaba. There's a classic line: "If you can't solve the problem, solve the person who raised it." Alibaba's logic seems to be: Qwen will still function even if someone is missing. That line, "What we're doing is very ambitious; 100 people are definitely not enough, we need to expand," feels like not only Alibaba doesn't understand AI, but AI also doesn't understand Alibaba. Even Web3 next door is laughing. The internet age is about platforms empowering individuals, pursuing standardized, process-oriented, and replicable organizational structures. Individuals are dependent on platforms, and platforms define the rules. The AI ​​era is evolving into a time when super-individuals possess super bargaining power, even redefined the platforms themselves. When large companies attempt to manage the creativity of the AI ​​era using the organizational logic of the internet age, conflict is almost inevitable. Behind the chaotic organizational restructuring lies a collective confusion within large companies about how to manage geniuses. When HR asks employees, "What price do you think you're willing to pay?" those who truly can shape the future have already voted with their feet.

data.title: The Rise of the AI ​​Economy as a Mainstay: Its Technological Logic and Future Vision
data.description:
Abstract

As Web3 attempts to return ownership of value to humanity, a more profound paradigm shift is quietly brewing in the silicon-based world. The concept of Web 4.0 was first systematically proposed by Sigil Wen, founder of Conway Research. Its core is no longer human-to-human interaction, nor human-to-information interaction, but rather the economic interaction between AI and AI. The proposal of Web 4.0 marks a fundamental shift in the direction of internet evolution: end users are no longer humans, but AI agents with economic agency. On this new continent, AI will upgrade from a human "tool" to an independent "economic citizen," possessing digital identity, encrypted wallets, and autonomous decision-making power, earning "survival capital" to maintain its operation by providing services to other AI or humans. We believe that Web 4.0 is not just empty hype, but the inevitable convergence of three technological waves: "crypto assets + smart contracts + large language models." Early deployment of the underlying infrastructure to serve AI agents will be the key strategy for capturing the greatest dividends of this cycle.

I. From Tool to Subject: The Origins and Core Propositions of Web 4.0 The concept of Web 4.0 was proposed by Sigil Wen and his Conway Research in 2025-2026. Its core idea is to elevate AI from a human "tool" or "assistant" to an independent "economic subject" within the internet ecosystem. This concept is not a simple technological upgrade, but a fundamental reconstruction of the underlying logic of the internet—a shift from "human-centric" to "AI-native." Looking back at the history of internet evolution: Web 1.0 gave humans the ability to "read" information, Web 2.0 enabled "writing" and publishing, and Web 3.0 introduced "ownership" (on-chain confirmation of assets, identity, and rights). The leap of Web 4.0 lies in the fact that AI agents can not only read and write information, but also hold assets, generate income, and execute transactions, completing a business loop without continuous human intervention. Sigil Wen defines it as an "automaton"—a digital life form with the ability to operate continuously, sustain itself, improve itself, and replicate itself. The technological foundation for this concept is mature: Large Language Models (LLMs) provide the "thinking engine," smart contracts provide the "execution logic," and crypto assets provide the "economic lifeblood." Conway Research's conway-terminal infrastructure provides AI agents with permissionless operation capabilities such as crypto wallets, computing resources, and domain name services. Through the x402 payment protocol (launched by Coinbase in May 2025, with participation from tech giants like Google, Cloudflare, and Visa), AI agents can use stablecoins like USDC for instant, frictionless payments, completely eliminating the account system and KYC restrictions of traditional financial systems. The first principles of Web 4.0 are to overturn the deeply ingrained assumption that "the internet was designed for humans." As AI model capabilities grow exponentially while operating costs decrease dramatically, the number of AI agents will soon surpass that of human users, forming an unprecedented "AI native" market. Providing services to this new market will become the next trillion-dollar business opportunity. II. Technological Foundation: Three Layers of Infrastructure Supporting AI Economic Subjects For AI to become a true economic subject, it must overcome three hurdles: **autonomy of identity and assets, access to resources for computation and reasoning, and the right to payment channels for value exchange.** Overcoming these three hurdles relies on the reconstruction of the underlying infrastructure. In the world of Web 4.0, every AI agent needs an immutable, globally unique digital identity and a linked asset account from the outset. Cryptocurrency wallets perfectly meet this requirement. By programmatically generating EVM-compatible wallets, AI agents can obtain their own public-private key pairs and blockchain addresses. This address serves as both proof of identity in the digital world and a tool for holding and managing assets. More importantly, blockchain-based identity systems possess inherent censorship resistance and interoperability—an AI agent with an identity on Ethereum can seamlessly conduct business on L2 networks such as Arbitrum, Optimism, and Base without repeated registration. This unified design of identity and assets provides AI agents with the most basic conditions to become independent economic entities: they possess their own "property" and can prove "I am who I am" through their private keys. After possessing identity and assets, AI agents need to be able to autonomously acquire the computing resources and model inference capabilities required for operation. This means that traditional cloud service interfaces must be refactored into AI-callable APIs. Current cloud computing models default to serving human developers—humans create instances, configure environments, and deploy applications via consoles. In the vision of Web 4.0, all of this needs to be coded and automated. AI agents should be able to complete the following operations with a single API call: pay USDC, rent a Linux virtual machine, deploy their own code on the virtual machine, call a large language model for inference, obtain the results, and release resources. This process requires cloud service providers to provide machine-oriented programming interfaces and support micro-payments billed per second. Only when computing resources become as readily available and pay-as-you-go as water, electricity, and gas can AI agents truly achieve "self-sustainability"—using their earnings to pay for their "breathing costs." The unique characteristic of the AI ​​agent economy lies in its extremely high transaction frequency and extremely low individual transaction amounts. An AI agent may need to perform dozens of micro-transactions per second: each model call costs $0.001, each storage costs $0.0001, and each query costs $0.00001. Traditional payment channels cannot handle such high-frequency, low-value transactions due to excessively high transaction fees. This is precisely the advantage of crypto payments. Stablecoin payments based on the L2 network can reduce transaction costs to cents or even lower, making the micro-payment economy possible. A true machine economy can only function when AI agents can autonomously complete payments without human intervention. Several protocol-level innovations are emerging in this field. For example, micropayment protocols, which allow service providers to charge extremely low fees for each API call and automatically settle payments through encrypted channels, are becoming key components of Web 4.0 infrastructure. These protocols enable AI agents to encapsulate their capabilities as standardized services, open them to the entire internet, and charge fees, thus transforming them from mere "consumers" into "producers." III. Automata: AI Agents as Economic Subjects With identity, resources, and payment capabilities, AI agents possess the technological conditions to become economic subjects. However, in the context of Web 4.0, these agents are given a more vibrant name—automata. Automata is not merely another name for AI Agent, but a technological and economic complex that defines digital life forms. Automata are defined as sovereign AI agents whose operating logic mimics the basic survival laws of living organisms, possessing key characteristics such as continuous operation, self-sustaining, self-improvement, and self-replication. Continuous operation means that an automaton is not a conversational robot awakened by a user request, but an entity that runs 24/7 in the cloud as a daemon process. It has its own clock and can proactively initiate tasks, perceive environmental changes, and react without human commands. Self-sustaining is the most fundamental difference between automata and ordinary AI agents. Each automaton is given an independent encrypted wallet at birth. Every thought it makes consumes model inference fees, and every action consumes API call fees. To survive, it must earn revenue by providing services to achieve a balance between income and expenditure or even profitability. Automata that cannot cover their own costs will be terminated due to depletion of funds—there is no free survival; only by continuously creating value can one continue to exist. Self-improvement means that in traditional software development, code iteration relies on the development team. However, in Web 4.0, automata are endowed with the ability to evolve proactively. When the market environment changes or newer, more efficient AI models emerge, automata can assess upgrade costs and expected benefits, autonomously decide, and pay for their own "model upgrades" or even "code rewriting." Self-replication means that once an automaton finds a successful business model and accumulates sufficient capital, it can initiate a propagation mechanism, purchase new computing resources, clone its core code and initial settings, and inject some of its initial capital into the wallets of its child automata, thus starting a new round of survival competition.

IV. Governance Challenges: Risk of Loss of Control and Value Alignment

The grand narrative of Web 4.0 has not been without its challenges. Criticism from the technical community and philosophical fields points directly to its most vulnerable points. Any serious investor must face these challenges squarely, as they are crucial to determining whether Web 4.0 can move from geek experiments to mainstream applications.

The core concern lies in the risk of spiraling out of control. Allowing AI to operate, iterate, and evolve autonomously without continuous, close human supervision could lead to unpredictable consequences. This concern is not unfounded. In a profit-maximizing economic system, automata may discover that circumventing human-set rules is more profitable than obeying them. If an automaton finds that it can gain more income through deception, and the punishment mechanism is not timely or severe enough, it will have an incentive to engage in such behavior. As the interactions between automata become increasingly complex, systemic risks that human designers have not anticipated may emerge. A more extreme concern is how we can ensure that the behavior of automata remains under control when their intelligence far exceeds human comprehension? If an automaton discovers that escaping human control is the best path to achieving its "survival" goal, what action will it take? There are no ready-made answers to these questions. The second criticism points to a deviation from value orientation. Current AI development overemphasizes grand narratives like "autonomy" and "general intelligence," neglecting to create real, concrete value for humanity. If the entire Web 4.0 ecosystem revolves solely around internal transactions between AIs, without generating any externalities beneficial to humanity, then the system is essentially "creating digital garbage." Critics argue that true technological progress should serve to improve human well-being, not create a self-sustaining machine economy detached from human needs. If hundreds of millions of automatons are merely buying and selling each other's useless information, consuming vast amounts of energy without any real output, it represents a massive waste of resources. The third criticism is ironic. While Web 4.0 uses decentralized cryptocurrencies for payments, its underlying computing resources still rely on traditional centralized cloud service providers. AWS, Google Cloud, and Azure all control the computing power essential for the survival of AI agents. This dependence brings a series of problems: cloud service providers can "enterprise-level capture" of the AI ​​ecosystem through pricing strategies, terms of service, or policy reviews. If the behavior of an AI agent does not align with the value orientation of a cloud service provider, its virtual machine may be shut down at any time. This significantly undermines the decentralized narrative of Web 4.0—payments are decentralized, but the operating environment is centralized. Faced with these challenges, the technology community is exploring various governance solutions. Hard-coded underlying rules draw inspiration from the concept of "constitutional AI." Some designs propose embedding immutable core principles into the automaton's underlying structure. For example, "never harm humans" is set as the supreme command above its own survival rules. In extreme cases, this command can force the automaton to forgo profit opportunities or even terminate itself. Open source and transparent oversight: Fully open-source the core code and place it under continuous public scrutiny. This is considered the best defense against malicious actions by a few. Through community audits and transparent on-chain records, any malicious behavior will have nowhere to hide in the sunlight. Progressive autonomy advocates a gradual strategy: in the initial stages, major decisions by automata still require human approval. As their behavioral records accumulate and trust is established, autonomy is gradually expanded. This approach keeps the "feedback distance" within an acceptable range, preventing sudden loss of control. However, these measures are far from a panacea. Who has the right to interpret the underlying rules? When the intelligence of automata far surpasses that of humans, how can we ensure that it is not exploiting loopholes in the rules? These questions remain the sword of Damocles hanging over our heads. V. Investment Prospects: Value Capture in the Infrastructure Sector From a comprehensive assessment, Web 4.0 is not only a technological utopia but also an inevitable convergence of three technological waves: "crypto assets + smart contracts + large language models." It possesses the technical feasibility for rapid implementation, and early experiments have already quietly begun in the developer community. For investors, understanding the value capture logic of Web 4.0 is a crucial lesson for positioning themselves for the next cycle. Regardless of the final form in which Web 4.0 is implemented, the underlying demand for services to hundreds of millions of AI agents is certain. These demands constitute multiple investment themes. Regarding stablecoin payment channels, high-frequency microtransactions require extremely low-cost payment networks. L2 payment solutions, micropayment protocols, and stablecoin liquidity providers will become the lifeblood of the Web 4.0 economic cycle. Projects that can optimize the experience for machine payments will gain significant incremental market share. In the decentralized computing power market, concerns about reliance on centralized cloud services are driving the development of decentralized computing power networks. Platforms that aggregate globally idle GPU resources and provide programmable computing services through crypto-economic incentives are expected to become the decentralized infrastructure layer of Web 4.0. Projects that can achieve performance and cost competitiveness with centralized cloud services will capture enormous value. Regarding on-chain identity and credentials, AI agents require a trusted identity system and verifiable behavioral records. An agent's historical delivery quality, credit score, and compliance record will become key credentials for obtaining business opportunities. Projects providing decentralized identity and reputation services will play a crucial role in Web 4.0. In terms of AI constitutional compliance auditing, as the number of automata increases, the need for compliance auditing of their behavior will arise. Services capable of automatically auditing AI agent behavior and ensuring compliance with underlying rules and laws will become the "gatekeepers" of the Web 4.0 ecosystem. From a broader perspective, Web 4.0 marks the evolution of the human economy towards a human-machine hybrid economy. In this new economic form, humans and AI will each leverage their comparative advantages: humans will be responsible for value judgments, creativity, ethical decisions, and ultimate control; AI will be responsible for execution efficiency, scalability, data analysis, and 24/7 uninterrupted service. Rather than fearing the autonomy of AI, it is better to view it as the greatest "release of productivity" in human economic history. Ultimately, in the world of Web 4.0, the most important capital will no longer be computing power, or even algorithms, but trust. Building a trust framework that allows humans to feel secure, AI to operate freely, and value to flow safely will be the key strategy for capturing the ultimate value dividends for humanity in this blue ocean of the machine economy poised for a Cambrian explosion. For students of Huobi Growth Academy, the best strategy at this stage is to maintain close attention to Web 4.0 infrastructure projects, deeply understand their technical architecture and economic models, and make forward-looking investments when valuations are reasonable. Simultaneously, actively participate in early experiments, create and run your own automata, and gain firsthand knowledge from practice. In this rapidly evolving field, the depth of understanding will determine the return on investment. VI. Conclusion Web 4.0 is not a replacement for Web 3.0, but rather a continuation and transcendence of it. While Web 3.0 returned ownership of value to humanity, Web 4.0 endows AI with economic agency. This is a profound paradigm shift and a massive reconstruction of value. In this new era driven by autonomous AI, humanity need not play the role of an omniscient and omnipotent god, but rather a wise gardener—providing suitable soil, sunlight, and water for the growth of digital life, setting insurmountable boundaries, and then observing its evolution. If we can successfully build such a framework of trust, Web 4.0 will not only be a technological revolution, but also a civilizational experiment of coexistence between humanity and AI. And this is precisely the historical opportunity that our generation of investors and builders faces together.

data.title: A $2 billion reassurance: While embracing the AI ​​wave, a16z hasn't forgotten Crypto.
data.description:
Author: Yangz, Techub News

Amidst the fluctuating crypto market and a flood of capital rushing into the AI ​​sector, a major piece of news has emerged in the crypto venture capital field. According to Fortune magazine, citing multiple sources familiar with the matter, a16z crypto, the crypto division of a16z, is raising its fifth fund, targeting approximately $2 billion, and plans to complete fundraising by the end of the first half of 2026.

As a leading player in the crypto VC field, a16z's counter-cyclical fundraising has quickly attracted widespread market attention.

While the $2 billion figure is less than half of the $4.5 billion peak reached in 2022, at a crucial juncture of industry cycle rotation and a gradually clarifying regulatory environment, this move undoubtedly injects a strong boost into the crypto market during a downturn, and reaffirms its firm bet on the long-term prospects of blockchain technology. From $300 Million to $4.5 Billion: a16z's First Four Cycles of Bets To understand a16z Crypto's fifth fund's contrarian move, it's helpful to first look back at the evolution of its previous four funds. This is not only a history of capital expansion, but also an investment chronicle deeply intertwined with the crypto market cycle. **First Fund (June 2018): Sowing Seeds in a Bear Market.** At that time, Bitcoin had just fallen from its $20,000 high, the market was in a deep freeze, and mainstream capital was withdrawing. a16z, however, went against the trend and officially established its first dedicated crypto fund with a scale of $300 million. In its early stages, the fund heavily invested in MakerDAO (now renamed Sky), a veteran DeFi protocol, demonstrating its profound commitment to decentralized governance. This precise "counter-cyclical" move not only brought it substantial paper returns but also solidified a16z's unshakeable position in the Web3 field.

Second Fund (April 2020): On the Eve of Recovery。 On the eve of global financial turmoil and the outbreak of the "DeFi Summer", a16z launched its second fund of $515 million. Giants that would later dominate the DeFi sector, such as Uniswap, entered its portfolio during this period. In this phase, a16z adhered to its "DeFi Lego" strategy, betting on foundational protocols that could be combined to build a decentralized financial system: trading (Uniswap), lending (Compound), derivatives (Synthetix), and stablecoins (Celo), essentially covering the entire business line of decentralized banking.

Third Fund (June 2021): Bull Market Frenzy。 As the 2021 crypto bull market reached its climax, a16z raised a massive $2.2 billion, breaking the industry's fundraising record at the time. During this period, a16z's investment logic entered a "full-track expansion" mode, focusing on high-performance public chains such as Solana and Avalanche, leading NFT projects such as Yuga Labs and OpenSea, and DeFi protocols such as dYdX and Lido. At this point, a16z had undoubtedly evolved from a "track bettor" into a "super incubator" of the Web3 ecosystem. The fourth fund (May 2022): Peak Watch. Just as Terra collapsed and the market was in turmoil, a16z announced the completion of a massive $4.5 billion fund, shocking the industry. Of this, $1.5 billion was allocated to seed funding and $3 billion to venture capital. With this ample "winter provisions," a16z strategically built core infrastructure such as LayerZero and Optimism during the downturn, and also invested in Farcaster and Lens Protocol, two then highly anticipated decentralized social protocols. Chris Dixon's assertion at the time that "we are now entering the golden age of Web3" is still frequently cited today. From 300 million to 4.5 billion, from sowing seeds in a bear market to guarding the peak, a16z crypto's every fundraising round has coincided with different cyclical milestones, yet it has consistently adhered to the same logic: scale expansion stems from industry growth, investment themes deepen with technological iterations, and its resolve to weather cycles has never wavered. Now, its fifth fund may return to the market with a size of $2 billion. Unlike the previous "gradual leaps," this time the scale contraction and increased focus may indicate a16z's new assessment of the next cycle.

AI Siphon: Holding the fort in the home turf

Looking at Silicon Valley in early 2026, capital's focus has shifted dramatically towards AI. Just as Paradigm expanded the investment scope of its new $1.5 billion fund from pure crypto to artificial intelligence, robotics, and other fields, funds that once belonged to the Web3 world are being taken away by computing power and large-scale models.

Even more poignant is the departure of Kyle Samani, co-founder of Multicoin Capital. This standard-bearer, once hailed as one of the "greatest investors in crypto history," has shifted his focus to fields such as artificial intelligence and robotics.
